title: Model Serving
lesson:
Model serving is the work of turning a trained model sitting in a folder into a live service that real people and apps can hit, around the clock, fast, and without falling over. Training builds the brain; serving is the front desk, the phone line, and the whole reception team that lets the world talk to that brain.

A model file alone does nothing useful. Someone has to load it onto a GPU, keep it warm, accept incoming requests, run them, and hand back the answer. That is the job of a serving engine (a program whose only purpose is to run a model efficiently for many callers at once).

Analogy 1: a restaurant kitchen

Imagine the model is a brilliant chef. Serving is everything around the chef that turns one cook into a working restaurant:

  • Waiters taking orders (the API layer).
  • A ticket rail where orders queue up.
  • A system that groups several orders so the chef cooks them together instead of one plate at a time (this grouping is batching).
  • A manager who hires a second chef when the dining room fills up (autoscaling).

The chef's skill never changes, but a great kitchen can serve 500 covers a night while a bad one serves 20 and burns half of them.

What actually happens on each request

A user sends some text. The serving engine turns it into tokens (the little chunks a model reads), runs them through the model on the GPU, and streams back generated tokens one by one.

The slow, expensive part is generation, because the model produces output one token at a time and must re-read the conversation each step. Serving engines exist to make that loop cheap. The two numbers everyone watches are latency (how long one user waits) and throughput (total tokens served per second across everyone). These pull against each other — see latency vs quality tradeoffs.

The tricks a serving engine uses

  • Continuous batching: the engine slots new requests into the running batch the moment a slot frees up, instead of waiting for a fixed group. Like a chef who adds a new ticket to the pan the second a dish leaves it.
  • KV cache management: the model remembers earlier tokens in a KV cache so it does not re-compute them. The engine packs many users' caches into GPU memory cleverly (vLLM's famous PagedAttention does this like an operating system managing RAM).
  • Fast kernels: optimised math like Flash Attention and tricks from inference optimization squeeze more out of each GPU.
  • Quantized weights: shipping the model in fewer bits via quantization so it fits and runs faster.

Analogy 2: a single laptop vs a power grid

Running a model on your own machine — see local inference with tools like Ollama — is like having a generator in your garage: great for you, but it cannot light a whole city.

Production serving is the power grid: many generators (GPUs), load balancers that route demand, spare capacity for the evening spike, and a way to add plants when the city grows. The same model can sit in both; the difference is everything wrapped around it.

What you choose when serving

For open-weight models, vLLM is the most common high-throughput engine in 2026; others include TGI and SGLang. You run it behind an API so apps talk to it over HTTP, usually in an OpenAI-compatible shape so client code is portable. Starting a vLLM server is roughly one command:

vllm serve meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct \
  --max-model-len 8192 \
  --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90 \
  --port 8000

Then any client hits it like a normal chat API:

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="x")
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Say hi in 5 words."}],
    stream=True,            # stream tokens as they are made
)
for chunk in resp:
    print(chunk.choices[0].delta.content or "", end="")

The big decision: build vs buy

You can rent cloud GPUs and run vLLM yourself (full control, you manage scaling and on-call), or call a hosted API and let someone else serve (no ops, but per-token cost and less control). Either way the same ideas apply: keep the GPU busy, batch aggressively, watch VRAM, and measure with real speed benchmarks.

💡 A handy mental split: time-to-first-token (how fast the reply starts, which users feel most) versus tokens-per-second (how fast it keeps flowing). Streaming makes a slow model feel fast by showing words as they appear.
mentalModel: The model is a chef; serving is the whole restaurant around it — waiters (the API), a ticket rail (the request queue), grouping orders to cook together (batching), and a manager who hires more chefs at the dinner rush (autoscaling). Same chef, vastly different number of happy diners.
mistakes: Confusing serving with training. Training teaches the model and is a one-off heavy job; serving runs the finished model for users, forever, and is judged on latency, throughput, and uptime — completely different concerns and hardware sizing.

Running one request at a time on an expensive GPU. A naive loop that handles a single user before starting the next leaves the GPU mostly idle. Without batching you might use 5% of a card you are paying full price for. Use a real serving engine.

Ignoring time-to-first-token and not streaming. Users judge speed by when words start appearing, not total time. A reply that streams in feels snappy; the same reply delivered in one silent lump after 8 seconds feels broken.

Forgetting the KV cache eats VRAM. Every concurrent user's conversation is stored in the KV cache. Long contexts and many users can blow past VRAM and crash the server, even though the model weights fit fine. Cap max-model-len and concurrency.

Sizing for average load, not peak. If you provision for the typical hour, the evening spike will queue requests and latency explodes. Plan headroom or set up autoscaling so extra GPUs spin up before users feel it.

Skipping a real load test. 'It worked when I tried it' tells you nothing about 200 simultaneous users. Always measure throughput and tail latency under realistic concurrency with proper speed benchmarking before launch.
exercise:
Spin up a serving engine and feel the difference batching makes. On a machine or rented cloud GPU, start a small model with vllm serve (use a 1B–8B instruct model). Hit it with a single streamed request from the OpenAI client and note the time-to-first-token. Then fire 20 requests at the same time (use a quick script with asyncio or even 20 parallel curl calls) and record total tokens-per-second across all of them. Compare the throughput of 1 request vs 20 — you should see total throughput rise sharply because the engine is batching them onto the GPU together.

Stretch goal: Lower --gpu-memory-utilization or raise --max-model-len until the server refuses or crashes under load, and explain in one sentence why the KV cache, not the model weights, was the thing that ran out of room.
solution: Hint: Single-request throughput is limited by one user's token stream, but the GPU is mostly idle during it. When you send 20 at once, continuous batching packs them into the same forward passes, so total tokens-per-second climbs far above 20× nothing — often several-fold over the single-request number — while each individual user's latency rises only modestly. For the stretch goal: the weights are a fixed cost loaded once, but every concurrent conversation needs its own slice of KV cache, and that cost grows with both context length and number of users. Push max-model-len or concurrency high enough and the cache outgrows free VRAM, so the engine either rejects requests or the process is killed — even though the model itself fit comfortably.
summary: Model serving is everything around a trained model that turns it into a fast, reliable, multi-user service: an API, a request queue, batching, KV-cache management, and scaling. The goals are low latency (one user's wait) and high throughput (total tokens served), and the main job is keeping the expensive GPU busy. In 2026 the default open-model engine is vLLM behind an OpenAI-compatible API; the core choice is run-it-yourself on cloud GPUs versus call a hosted endpoint.
takeaways: Serving wraps a trained model in an API, a queue, batching, and scaling.
The goal: keep the costly GPU busy — high throughput, low latency.
vLLM behind an OpenAI-compatible API is the 2026 default for open models.

diagram.caption: A serving engine merges separate user requests into one batched GPU pass, then streams each reply back.

quiz.q: What problem does a serving engine like vLLM mainly solve?
options: Teaching the model new facts from your data
Running the trained model efficiently for many simultaneous users
Converting the model's weights into a smaller file format
Labelling and cleaning your training dataset
quiz.explain: Serving runs an already-trained model for live traffic, keeping the GPU busy across many callers. Teaching new facts is fine-tuning; shrinking weights is quantization.

quiz.q: Users complain the chatbot 'feels slow' even though total reply time is fine. What should you fix first?
options: Buy a bigger model with more parameters
Disable batching so each user gets a dedicated GPU
Increase max-model-len to allow longer answers
quiz.explain: Perceived speed is driven by when words start appearing. Streaming and a fast time-to-first-token make a reply feel snappy even if total tokens-per-second is unchanged.

quiz.q: Your model weights fit comfortably in VRAM, but the server crashes once many users with long chats connect. The likeliest cause is:
options: The KV cache for all those concurrent conversations exhausted VRAM
The model weights silently doubled in size at runtime
Streaming uses too much CPU and starves the GPU
The OpenAI-compatible API can only hold one request at a time
quiz.explain: Weights are a fixed one-time cost, but every concurrent conversation needs its own KV cache that grows with context length and user count, so it can outgrow free VRAM and crash the server.

quiz.q: Which statement about continuous batching is correct?
options: It waits for a fixed-size group before running anything on the GPU
It runs each request fully before accepting the next one
It slots new requests into the in-flight batch as soon as a slot frees up
It only works for training, not for inference
quiz.explain: Continuous batching adds new requests to the batch already running, the moment a slot opens — unlike static batching, which waits for a full fixed group, leaving the GPU idle.

keyTerms.def: The job of running a trained model as a live service that accepts requests from apps and users and returns answers fast and reliably.
A program built only to run a model efficiently for many callers at once, handling batching, the KV cache, and GPU memory. vLLM, TGI, and SGLang are examples.
Total work served per second — usually measured in tokens per second across all users combined. High throughput means the GPU is being used well.
How long one user waits. Often split into time-to-first-token (when the reply starts) and tokens-per-second (how fast it then flows).
Slotting new requests into a batch already running on the GPU as soon as a slot frees, instead of waiting for a fixed group — keeps the GPU full.
The delay before the very first output token appears. It is what users feel most, and streaming hides slowness by showing words as they are made.
Sending output tokens to the client one at a time as the model produces them, so the answer appears to type itself instead of arriving in one lump.
Automatically adding or removing GPUs (servers) as demand rises and falls, so you have enough capacity at peak without paying for idle machines off-peak.
Exposing your own server using the same request and response shape as OpenAI's API, so existing client code works against it with only a changed base URL.
